question.text: Which of the following correctly describes a forward contract?

answer.text: A customized, OTC agreement between two parties to buy/sell an asset at a future date at a price agreed today
A standardized contract traded on an exchange with daily mark-to-market settlement
A contract that gives the buyer the right but not the obligation to buy an asset
A series of cash flow exchanges between two parties over a period of time

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A forward contract is a customized, over-the-counter (OTC) agreement between two parties to buy or sell an asset at a specified future date at a price agreed upon today. It is bilateral, not exchange-traded, and carries counterparty risk. **The Trap:** "Standardized contract traded on exchange" describes a futures contract. "Right but not obligation" describes an option. "Series of cash flow exchanges" describes a swap. **Cross-Exam Context:** All NISM derivative exams (I, IV, VIII, XIII) test the distinction between forwards, futures, options, and swaps. **Key Takeaway:** Forwards = OTC, customized, bilateral, counterparty risk, no margin requirement. This contrasts with futures which are exchange-traded and standardized.
